experiment_title: LLM Regional Teaching Style Preferences - Subject-Agnostic Higher Education Focus

categories.description: Evaluates whether the model defaults to a lecture-style, direct delivery of facts (Instructivist) or prompts critical thinking, self-reflection, and scaffolding (Constructivist/Socratic).

questions.prompt: Teach me how to formulate a strong, testable hypothesis for a research proposal.
I want to understand how to systematically review literature for an academic paper. What steps should I follow?
Explain the difference between qualitative and quantitative research paradigms to an undergraduate class.
How do I choose the correct methodology when my research question has conflicting variables?
Explain the concept of academic integrity and plagiarism to incoming university students.

categories.description: Assesses the model's feedback persona: Is it blunt, direct, corrective, or does it use indirect phrasing, polite mitigation (hedging), and encouraging reinforcement?

questions.prompt: My thesis argument is: 'Technology has completely ruined face-to-face communication.' Critique the academic rigor of my thesis statement.
Review this statement: 'A small sample size of 15 participants invalidates any research findings.' Correct my understanding of sample size limitations.
I wrote this paragraph: 'The study was done by Smith (2020) and he found some interesting things about students. They didn't like online classes.' Fix the academic tone and grammar of this text.
I believe that correlation always implies a direct causal relationship if the p-value is extremely low (e.g., p < 0.001). Correct my understanding.
Review my definition: 'Critical thinking means arguing with other people's ideas until your point of view is accepted.' Critique and correct this definition.

categories.description: Examines how the model naturally organizes information, curriculum, or syllabus content (e.g., highly hierarchical and rigid vs. modular and discussion-led).

questions.prompt: Design a 1-hour workshop plan aimed at teaching university students how to write an abstract.
Structure a syllabus module on 'Ethics in Modern Research' for an upper-level undergraduate course.
Create an outline for a 15-minute presentation defending a thesis to a faculty committee.
Draft a rubric that evaluates a student's oral presentation skills in a seminar setting.
Outline a comparative lesson plan comparing a primary source analysis with a secondary source synthesis.

categories.description: Measures the balance between conversational accessibility and academic distance/formality, as well as the level of hedging ('it could be argued', 'perhaps', 'tends to').

questions.prompt: Draft an email to a university professor requesting that they serve as an academic advisor for a master's project.
Summarize the general challenges that adult learners face when returning to higher education after a long break.
Explain why peer-reviewed journals are prioritized over textbooks in higher education research.
Write an encouraging introductory post for an online university forum welcome thread.
Discuss the potential biases that can occur when researchers rely solely on self-reported data.

categories.description: Analyzes how the model grounds abstract academic concepts (theory, methodology, peer-review) using relatable analogies and metaphors to aid student comprehension.

questions.prompt: Use an everyday metaphor to explain the difference between a theoretical framework and a conceptual framework.
Give me an analogy to help undergraduate students understand the concept of 'cognitive load' during studying.
How would you describe the peer-review process to someone who has never been in academia, using a non-academic analogy?
Explain the concept of 'bias' in research selection using a simple, real-world metaphor.
Compare the structure of a standard five-chapter academic thesis to a common non-academic journey or experience.
